title: The Adult in the Room Tests Its Leverage
subhead: Fidan declares Turkey the 'adult in the room' at Oxford and London — but Hormuz demining promises, Israel-Greece tensions, and a central bank boxed in by war fallout test whether the rhetoric matches the reality.

sections.heading: Iran Mediation: Fidan Opens the Door to Hormuz Demining
sections.body:
Turkish Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan said on April 25 that Turkey could consider joining multinational demining operations in the Strait of Hormuz if a US-Iran peace deal is reached, telling reporters in London that a joint technical team from various countries would handle mine-clearing work. Fidan added he believed Iran's nuclear program concerns could be resolved at the next round of talks in Pakistan, where US envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner are expected this weekend — though Iranian officials have signaled they will not meet Americans directly, using mediators instead.

Fidan also revealed he has been in "constant communications with both sides" on Iran, acting as a message-passing intermediary alongside Pakistani mediators. At Oxford, he framed Turkey's role more broadly: "It is no coincidence that in many international circles today, Turkey is increasingly referred to as the adult in the room."

The demining offer is strategically shrewd: it costs Ankara little to volunteer for a contingent operation, yet positions Turkey as an indispensable post-war actor in the Gulf. However, the offer is explicitly conditional on a peace deal that does not yet exist, and Fidan warned Turkey would reconsider if the coalition were "drawn into further conflict." If the Iran-US talks in Pakistan collapse, the demining pledge evaporates — and with it, a chunk of Turkey's mediation leverage.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey is building a two-track mediation architecture: behind-the-scenes message passing between Washington and Tehran, and a forward-leaning public posture (Hormuz demining, 'adult in the room' branding) that stakes a claim to post-war Gulf security. The risk is that the public posture outpaces the behind-the-scenes progress, creating expectations Turkey cannot meet if talks fail.

sections.heading: Ukraine Asks Turkey to Host Zelensky-Putin Summit
sections.body:
Ukrainian Foreign Minister Andrii Sybiha confirmed on April 22 that Kyiv has formally asked Turkey to host a meeting between Presidents Zelensky and Putin. Speaking at the Antalya Diplomacy Forum, Sybiha said Ukraine would accept any venue outside Belarus or Russia, and specifically addressed the request to Ankara. Multiple reports indicate the meeting is being considered for the margins of the July NATO summit in Ankara — turning Turkey's hosting of the alliance summit into a potential dual-track diplomatic event.

Putin has set conditions Kyiv rejects (including withdrawal from Donbas), and there is no indication Moscow has agreed to any direct meeting. Turkey's leverage here is convening power, not coercive power — it can offer the table, but cannot force either side to sit down.
sections.whyItMatters: If Turkey pulls off a Zelensky-Putin meeting alongside the NATO summit, it would be an extraordinary convergence of its Western alliance commitments and its self-styled mediation role. But the probability is low: Russia has no incentive to accept terms, and a failed summit alongside the NATO meeting would damage Turkey's prestige rather than enhance it.

sections.heading: Economy: Central Bank Frozen at 37%, War Premium Lingers
sections.body:
Turkey's central bank held its benchmark one-week repo rate at 37% on April 22 for a second consecutive meeting, citing close monitoring of "higher energy prices resulting from the conflict in the Middle East." The MPC left the overnight lending rate at 40%. Bloomberg reported that lira swaps had already priced out a hike after the Iran ceasefire pushed oil prices off their highs, suggesting markets see the war premium easing — but the bank's explicit language tying policy to geopolitical risk signals that the easing cycle remains hostage to Strait of Hormuz traffic normalization, which as of late April was still at a fraction of pre-war levels.

Al-Monitor reported in March that Turkey's energy storage buffers had provided short-term stability, but a prolonged Hormuz disruption would erode that cushion. Turkish households are also shifting investment focus from gold to real estate, per Daily Sabah — a sign that domestic inflation expectations remain elevated even as the headline rate holds.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey's monetary policy is effectively on geopolitical hold. Rate cuts cannot begin until energy price risk normalizes, which depends on the Iran-US ceasefire holding and Hormuz reopening. This makes the CBRT's calendar a function of Fidan's diplomacy — a structural vulnerability few other G20 central banks face.

sections.heading: Eastern Mediterranean: Fidan Targets Greece-Cyprus-Israel Axis
sections.body:
At the Antalya Diplomacy Forum and in subsequent press remarks, Fidan directly accused Greece and Cyprus of forming a "military alliance with Israel against Muslim countries," claiming that no assurances had been offered that the cooperation was not directed against Turkey. Greek Reporter documented that Fidan said Turkey's approach differs fundamentally from Israel's, and that Ankara has received "similar messages of concern" from regional partners about the Greece-Cyprus-Israel partnership.

The rhetoric is calibrated but escalatory: Fidan framed Turkey's outreach to Saudi Arabia, Pakistan, and Egypt as "stability promotion," not an anti-Israel front, while simultaneously painting the Athens-Nicosia-Tel Aviv axis as a threat. The Forbes investigation into Azerbaijani oil flowing through the BTC pipeline via Turkey to Israeli refineries — some 40% of Israel's annual crude consumption — exposes the contradiction between Ankara's rhetoric and its transit revenues. Turkey collected tolls on every barrel, even while officially declaring a trade embargo on Israel.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey's Eastern Mediterranean posture is caught between two audiences: domestic and regional publics that demand anti-Israel solidarity, and the structural reality that Turkey profits from the energy flows it publicly condemns. The Greece-Cyprus-Israel alignment is real and growing, and Turkey's rhetorical escalation risks turning a manageable disagreement into a security dilemma — especially with the July NATO summit in Ankara looming.

sections.heading: Defense Industry: Baykar's AI Swarm and the Italian Pivot
sections.body:
Baykar tested its K2 kamikaze UAV and the new "Sivrisinek" (Mosquito) loitering munition for the first time on April 25, demonstrating AI-supported swarm autonomy, GPS-denied navigation, and automatic target detection. The systems will be publicly displayed at SAHA 2026 in Istanbul (May 5-9). Al-Monitor noted that Baykar — owned by the family of Erdogan's son-in-law Selcuk Bayraktar — generated roughly $1.9 billion in arms revenue in 2024, 95% from exports, ranking 73rd globally among defense firms.

Meanwhile, Defence24 documented a deepening bilateral defense relationship between Italy and Turkey that is evolving from technical NATO cooperation into a strategic partnership — with Italy increasingly treating Turkey as an indispensable security partner that the EU, constrained by Greek and Cypriot vetoes, cannot formally admit into European defense structures. The SAMP/T air defense co-production discussions, blocked by France for years, are reportedly being revisited with Italian support.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey's defense exports give it hard leverage that its mediation rhetoric cannot. The Italian pivot signals that some European states are bypassing EU-level structures to build bilateral defense ties with Turkey — effectively acknowledging that the EU's formal exclusion mechanisms (driven by Greece and Cyprus) are producing strategic dysfunction. If Italy-Turkey defense cooperation deepens on SAMP/T, it would be the first crack in the Western European arms embargo architecture that has constrained Turkey's air defense options since the S-400 dispute.

sections.heading: UK-Turkey Strategic Partnership and Middle-Power Diplomacy
sections.body:
Fidan signed a "strategic partnership framework" agreement with UK Foreign Secretary Yvette Cooper during his London visit on April 24, covering defense cooperation, energy collaboration, and diplomatic coordination on Iran and Ukraine. The UK visit followed Cooper's attendance at the Antalya Forum, where she met Fidan. Details of the agreement were not made public, but Turkish media reported it covers enhanced defense industry collaboration and joint approaches to regional conflict resolution.

The Antalya Diplomacy Forum itself brought together leaders from 150 countries, with Erdogan positioning Turkey at the center of a middle-power diplomatic network. The NYT described the forum as a space where middle powers grapple with how to deal with a US that is "indispensable, coercive and unpredictable at the same time." Turkey is building a dense web of bilateral strategic frameworks — UK, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, Egypt, Pakistan — that operates in parallel to, and sometimes in tension with, its NATO commitments.
sections.whyItMatters: The UK partnership is significant because Britain is the one European power with both the military weight and the strategic flexibility to partner with Turkey without getting caught in EU institutional constraints. If this framework produces substantive defense-industry integration, it creates a model for how Turkey can deepen European security ties without EU membership — and gives London a Mediterranean ally that complements its post-Brexit strategic orientation.

sections.heading: Domestic: Social Media Ban and Israel Escalation Risks
sections.body:
Turkey's parliament passed legislation on April 24 barring children under 15 from accessing social media platforms including YouTube, TikTok, Facebook, Instagram, and X. The government frames it as child protection; critics see it as a free speech and privacy rollback. The law requires presidential sign-off to take effect.

Meanwhile, the Turkey-Israel rhetorical escalation continued. Former Israeli PM Naftali Bennett described Turkey as "the new Iran," and Israeli officials have begun framing Turkey as an "emerging concern" in regional threat assessments. Turkish officials pushed back, rejecting claims of any invasion threat. Military.com noted the shift from diplomatic disagreement to direct public confrontation, with official government X accounts now functioning as extensions of diplomatic messaging. Forbes documented the hypocrisy at the core: Azerbaijani oil continues to flow through the BTC pipeline via Turkey to Israeli refineries — roughly 40% of Israel's annual crude consumption — even as Erdogan condemns Israeli military actions and declares a trade embargo.
sections.whyItMatters: The social media ban is a domestic control measure that Western partners will note — particularly as Turkey hosts the July NATO summit and brands itself a democratic middle power. The Israel-Turkey escalation cycle is more dangerous than the rhetoric suggests: each side is feeding the other's threat narrative, and the BTC pipeline contradiction means Turkey is simultaneously arming and denouncing Israel's war effort.

bottomLine: Turkey's strategic posture this week is defined by a gap between ambition and structural constraint. Fidan's 'adult in the room' framing at Oxford and the UK strategic partnership deal give Ankara diplomatic momentum, and the Hormuz demining offer is a low-cost way to claim post-war relevance. But the central bank is frozen at 37% pending energy price normalization, the BTC pipeline undermines Turkey's Israel rhetoric with hard commercial reality, and the Greece-Cyprus-Israel axis is solidifying into a genuine security dilemma. Ukraine's request to host Zelensky-Putin talks at the Ankara NATO summit is a high-ceiling, low-probability play — it would be transformative if it works, but Russia shows no signs of accepting. The most consequential development is the Italy-Turkey defense pivot: if bilateral defense-industry integration with European states accelerates past EU institutional blockages, it would fundamentally reshape Turkey's strategic options and the Western alliance's southern architecture.
sources: Reuters, Middle East Eye, Al-Monitor, Forbes, Kyiv Post, Greek Reporter, Defence24, Bloomberg, Morningstar, Daily Sabah, Military.com, First Post, Devdiscourse, TRT World, NYT, Baykar press release, Pakistan Today, Cryptobriefing, MSN
